content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 24, 2012


Michael DeArton
Law Office of Michael DeArton, P.C.
PO Box 2742
Redding, CA 96009-2742

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-11-239

Dear Mr. DeArton:

This letter responds to your request for advice, on behalf of Mr. Robert J. Laacke,
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This
letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the
Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby
(1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature and not limited to a
specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

Please note that the Commission does not provide advice on bodies of law outside the
confines of the Act. Thus, we offer no opinion on the application of other incompatible
activities and conflict-of-interest laws that may apply including, but not limited to, common
law conflict of interest and Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

Does Mr. Laacke, a member of the Surprise Valley Healthcare District Board of
Directors, have a conflict of interest under the Act considering his financial support of a
private medical practice including the donation of office space to the practice?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code
of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations,
unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-11-239
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

While Mr. Laacke is subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, the Act does
not prohibit Mr. Laacke from sitting on the Surprise Valley Healthcare District Board of
Directors while financially supporting a private medical practice. We do, however, caution
that Mr. Laacke is prohibited as a public official from making, participating in making, or
using his official position to influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any economic interests he may have.

FACTS

Mr. Laacke is an elected member of the Board of Directors for the Surprise Valley
Healthcare District (the "SVHD"), which is located in the City of Cedarville on the eastern
edge of Modoc County. The SVHD is a rural healthcare district organized under Health and
Safety Code Section 32000 et seq. Historically, between two to four physicians are on the
SVHD medical staff. Other staff for the SVHD, including nursing and administration, totals
approximately 70 people. The SVHD facilities include a skilled nursing facility, an
emergency room, and a primary care clinic.

Additionally, Mr. Laacke owns a large home within the City of Cedarville. At the
time of purchase, this home had been divided into four residential apartments. At his own
expense, Mr. Laacke has converted one of the apartments to serve as a private physician's
office and obtained a permit allowing for this use. Mr. Laacke is currently allowing
Dr. Chuck Colas to occupy this office space rent free. Pursuant to their understanding,
Dr. Colas will be permitted to occupy the office rent free so long as Mr. Laacke is serving on
the SVHD Board of Directors and for one year and one day thereafter. In addition to the free
office space, Mr. Laacke has provided Dr. Colas with initial material and financial support in
establishing his private medical practice.

In regard to Mr. Laacke's support of Dr. Colas, Mr. Laacke has no right or
expectation to be paid back or receive any return on investment. The only condition placed
on Mr. Laacke's support was that it be used by Dr. Colas to further medical care within their
remote area of Modoc County. Mr. Laacke has no ownership interest, management
authority, advisory role, or other interest in Dr. Colas's practice. Moreover, Mr. Laacke will
not receive or claim any tax deduction, nor receive any services from Dr. Colas. Dr. Colas
bears all further costs and long term risks of his practice and is exclusively entitled to any
profit from the practice.

ANALYSIS

While Mr. Laacke is subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, the Act does
not prohibit Mr. Laacke from sitting on the SVHD Board of Directors while financially
supporting Dr. Colas's private practice. Pursuant to Section 87100 of the Act, Mr. Laacke is,
however, prohibited as a public official from making, participating in making, or using his

File No. I-11-239
Page No. 3


official position to influence a governmental decision in which he has a financial interest. A
public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning of
the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18700(a).)

Generally, to determine whether Mr. Laacke has a disqualifying conflict of interest in
any particular governmental decision, Mr. Laacke must first identify his economic interests
that may be affected by the decision. Economic interests from which a conflict of interest
may arise are defined in Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5 and include:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which
the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b)).

• An economic interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)

• An economic interest in a source of income to the official, including promised
income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)³

• An economic interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $420
or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); Regulation
18703.4.)

• An economic interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the
official's immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

From the facts provided, Mr. Laacke is donating office space during the term of his
position with the SVHD Board of Directors, and for one year and one day thereafter, to
Dr. Colas to help establish Dr. Colas's practice. Moreover, it appears that Mr. Laacke's
donation is purely charitable as you have stated that Mr. Laacke is not receiving any benefit
from his support of Dr. Colas including any tax deduction, services, or income and that
Dr. Colas is exclusively entitled to any profit from the business. Based upon these facts,
Mr. Laacke does not appear to have any economic interest in the private practice of
Dr. Colas, as either a business entity or a source of income, potentially disqualifying him
from any particular governmental decision.

³ We note that a public official's income includes income that has been promised to the public official,
but not yet received by him or her, if he or she has a legally enforceable right to the promised income.
(Regulation 18703.3(a)(1).)

File No. I-11-239
Page No. 4


However, at this time, you have not provided a specific factual situation in which a
particular governmental decision is implicated. Accordingly, we can only caution that there
is a potential conflict of interest any time Mr. Laacke assumes a governmental decision-
making role where it is possible that the decision in question will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any of his economic interests. For example, you have
indicated that Mr. Laacke owns the property, which he allows Dr. Colas to use, and that this
property consists of several rental units. From these facts, Mr. Laacke appears to have and
an economic interest in this real property and potentially has additional economic interests in
his rental business, as both a source of income and as a business entity, and in the paying
tenants of the property, as sources of income. Under the Act, Mr. Laacke is disqualified from
making, participating in making, or influencing any decision by the SVHD with a reasonably
foreseeable material financial affect on any of these economic interests.

To assist Mr. Laacke in determining whether he has a disqualifying conflict-of-
interest in any particular decision before the SVHD, we have enclosed the Commission fact
sheet entitled, "Can I Vote? Overview of Conflicts Laws," which outlines the standard eight-
step conflict-of-interest analysis. Should Mr. Laacke have any additional questions regarding
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act as they relate to a specific governmental
decision, it is advisable that he seek further assistance providing all relevant facts pertaining
to the decision.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By:   Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

Enclosure
